segments.text: Have you ever heard of a split-flap
display before? Well, you've probably
seen one even if you didn't know that's
what it was called. These things used to
be in airports and train stations
everywhere, but I'm a little bit too
young to have seen them there. So,
nowadays you'll most often see them at
bars and restaurants, fancy startup
offices, and places like that. I
stumbled upon one in a bookstore and
ever since I saw it in person,
>> [music]
>> I've wanted one of my own.
But, did I buy one?
Hell no. I just spent hundreds of hours
building this thing for myself. And in
this video, I'm going to show you the
entire process from start to finish of
how this thing came to be. If you stick
around to the end, I might just let you
control it yourself.
My obsession with split flaps started
when I made this little eight-character
model from a tutorial by Morgan Manly.
But, hold on a second. Let me rewind.
If you don't already know what a
split-flap display is, let me show you
how one works using this clear acrylic
open-source model from a project by
Scott Bessac. Inside of a split-flap
module such as this, you'll find a hub
that looks a little something like this
that has a number of holes on either
side. Inside of those holes rides one of
these flaps. [music]
These are half flaps or split flaps, if
you will. And once they rotate down due
to [music] gravity,
they'll display a whole character. Once
we turn it on, you can see the module
stepping here one at a time. You can see
the hub rotating in there with the help
of the stepper motor. You can also see
at the top here the flaps are retained
[music] by this faceplate so that only
one flips down at a time.
The type of motor you're using on a
split-flap display is important. This is
a stepper motor and it allows us to turn
a precise number of degrees so that we
can display whatever letter we want.
The other half of the story is this
little sensor here, which is called a
Hall effect sensor.
And when it rotates, it can detect this
little magnet here
so that when I plug it in,
it's going to rotate until the magnet is
in line with the Hall effect sensor,
which is indicated by that LED there.
And then after that, it knows precisely
where it is. And if you want to type a
C, for example, it knows how many
degrees to rotate to show that C. So,
this is just how one module of a
split-flap display works, but imagine
you had 50 of them. It gets more
complicated as you add more of these and
that's the project that I'll be working
on in this video.
All right. Now that we all know how a
split-flap works, let me show you this
one again. I'll take it apart, show you
some of the things I like and dislike,
and that way we'll get some requirements
going for a new design. So, right off
the bat, one thing I really like about
this design is the size of the modules
themselves. They're pretty compact. The
motor is right inside of the hub, which
I'll show you in a second. So, it allows
them to be right up next to each other,
but I don't like how many modules it
has. It only has eight in this version
and I definitely want mine to have a lot
more than that. So, let me take it apart
and show you some other things that I
want to change on the inside. All right.
Now that it's apart, you can see all
these modules are strung together by
connectors here
and they're all held together by these
long threaded rods that go through all
eight modules. This is one thing I don't
like cuz it's hard to put these together
and requires you to, you know, sandwich
all these wires together with these
spacers and it was just a pain to put
together and I don't think it's going to
scale very well when I add more modules.
So, we're definitely going to change
that. Here's a closer view of one of the
modules' enclosures and I really like
most of this design. The motor is in
here. Here's some gears that help turn
the hub. There's a Hall effect sensor in
the back and I like this wire retainer
clip. So, we're definitely going to use
this as a starting point. But, one thing
I don't like too much is this
electronics package here. It's a bit of
a rat's nest of wires and it was hard to
solder it all together and cram it in
there. So, we're definitely going to try
and improve upon that for my version.
Our first criteria, I think, is that we
want it to have more flaps. I like A
through Z, 0 through 9, but [music] I
want some special characters involved. I
like the ones that involve some color.
So, we're going to set it to 64 flaps.
We also want it to have more modules in
the first place. [music] I don't want
just a single line. My goal is going to
be 15 modules per row and three rows
[music] for a total of 45 modules. Now,
with that many modules, we need it to be
much easier to build. [music] So, the
goal is for it to be totally modular and
essentially that means snap-on, snap-off
design. I don't want to have any
threaded rods throughout the entire
thing. I don't want to deal with taking
the whole [music] thing apart just to
service one in the middle. I just want
to be able to plug and play and have the
modules working. [music] For criterion
number four, I don't want to have to do
too much soldering. I think there might
be a little bit involved, but for the
most part, I want the boards to be
self-enclosed. I want to just be able to
plug in the motor, plug in the sensor,
and be ready to go. And the last
criteria, might be the hardest to hit,
is that I want to spend under $1,000 on
this project. Online, I can buy a board
that's 15 by 3 [music] for under $1,000.
So, I think it would only be fitting
that mine should cost less than that. It
wouldn't make any sense for me to build
one [music] just for it to be more
expensive than the one that I could buy.
So, that's my goal. That's my target
that I'm going to hit. We'll keep a
tally of how [music] much I spend and
see if I get there in the end.
All right. Let's tackle the easy part
first. How are we going to make it have
64 flaps? Well, the current version has
37. So, to make it have 64, you might
think you just need to add 27 more
holes.
But, it can't be that easy, can't it?
Well, the short answer is maybe.
These are the stepper motors that power
the whole thing. They're little 28BYJ-48
5-V stepper motors and they don't have a
great resolution. So, there's no
guarantee they can handle 64 flaps. But,
there's nothing to do than to just try
it out. So, I went into Onshape, removed
all the old holes, and extruded 64 new
ones. But a bing, but a boom, you got a
new drum.
And after a quick 3D print, here's the
old one and here's the new one. You can
see the 27 new holes. They're a little
bit smaller and I did have to modify the
flaps to make them a little bit thinner
and smaller to fit, but that's pretty
much all I had to do. Just add the new
characters [music] that we want and all
we got to do is try it on a module and
see if there's enough resolution to make
it work. And thankfully, it did work.
You can see it step along happily here
and it gives me more confidence that
these things have enough resolution to
handle the 64 flaps. I guess it was just
as easy as adding more holes to the
drum. And now we can move on to the next
step, which is probably going to be a
lot harder.
Okay, it's time to tackle the
electronics, which is one of the parts
I'm most concerned about cuz I have the
least experience in this, but I think
I've [music] got a plan.
So, the way the last one worked is that
there was a single ESP32 microcontroller
controlling [music] all eight motors.
But, for a variety of reasons that I'm
not going to get into in this video, I
don't think that's going to scale up
very well when we have to control 45
[music] motors and read 45 sensors. So,
instead, what I think I'm going to do is
put an individual chip on each module
that can just control that module's
motor.
And then we'll have a Raspberry Pi
sending signals to all of them telling
them where to go. It sounds pretty
simple, but it will entail me having to
make a custom PCB, which is something
I've never done before. So, I'll give it
my best shot. Okay, check this out. This
is what I came up with. There's a
microcontroller there, motor controller
there, plugins for the motor and the
Hall effect sensor. It looks a bit
shotty, but it's my first time doing
this. So, I just want to make sure the
idea is there. So, I'm going to send
this to the manufacturer and see if it
works when it comes back. One week
later.
All right. Check it out. Here's the PCB
all made up. Got all the components on
there and I went ahead and did some
testing. All right. I got it all
programmed up, plugged in the power and
the motor plugged in and when I turn it
on,
there we go. You can see the motor
spinning.
Overall, these things worked pretty
well. There was a resistor on there that
I had to remove, but other than that,
nothing too wrong with the design.
There are a couple things we still got
to figure out like how to deliver the
power and the signal to it when it's all
on the board, but I'm pretty happy with
the results and I'm going to change a
few things and [music] then hopefully we
can manufacture 50 of them.
I think I found the perfect solution for
both mounting the modules and delivering
the power and data they'll need. It
starts with this. This is DIN rail and
it's a standardized type of rail that's
often used for mounting electrical
components.
And I also found this cool 3D print
online that has a compliant mechanism
that allows me to just snap right onto
the DIN rails and I can stick the
modules on there. They'll be good
[music] to go.
I don't know how well you can see, but
there's also four holes that I put
inside this mount and those [music] are
going to be for pogo pins. So, I've
taken the liberty of making this new
circuit board, which is essentially just
four long traces that will carry the
power, ground, [music] and signal A and
B lines. And the pogo pins will
interface directly with that. So, all
that's left to do is to modify the
circuit boards to have the pads to fit
the pogo pins that will fit inside the
mount here and I should be able [music]
to order 50 more and we can start
actually building the thing. All right.
I just got home from a couple weeks away
for the holidays, but look what's here.
It's about midnight and I do have to go
to bed, but I needed to open this and
check out what's inside.
Ooh.
Nice. I completely forgot, to be honest,
that I got them in blue.
Looks like there's all 50.
How cool is that?
All right. Well,
to be honest, I didn't think they would
have these
edges on them, but hopefully they'll
just snap off.
Sweet.
All right. So, I just got to get the

get them all programmed up and plugged

Cool. All right. I'm going to bed, but
I'll work on these tomorrow. Here's a

Up here in the top right is the actual

going to be able to receive and

You got the motor controller chip and a

four pins in the middle are where the
pogo pins are going to I
So, I'm actually pretty proud of this

I just think it's so cool how you can go

you know, not too long later have 50

work in your hands. And I'm really glad

anyway, let's get on to building the

We're finally at the point where we can

So, we're going to try and speed run it

All right, ready,

All right, first we're going to be

[music]

Got my work cut out for me, though.
Excited to test this thing out. During

Each set of flaps was its own, so I had

full_text: Have you ever heard of a split-flap display before? Well, you've probably seen one even if you didn't know that's what it was called. These things used to be in airports and train stations everywhere, but I'm a little bit too young to have seen them there. So, nowadays you'll most often see them at bars and restaurants, fancy startup offices, and places like that. I stumbled upon one in a bookstore and ever since I saw it in person, >> [music] >> I've wanted one of my own. But, did I buy one? Hell no. I just spent hundreds of hours building this thing for myself. And in this video, I'm going to show you the entire process from start to finish of how this thing came to be. If you stick around to the end, I might just let you control it yourself. My obsession with split flaps started when I made this little eight-character model from a tutorial by Morgan Manly. But, hold on a second. Let me rewind. If you don't already know what a split-flap display is, let me show you how one works using this clear acrylic open-source model from a project by Scott Bessac. Inside of a split-flap module such as this, you'll find a hub that looks a little something like this that has a number of holes on either side. Inside of those holes rides one of these flaps. [music] These are half flaps or split flaps, if you will. And once they rotate down due to [music] gravity, they'll display a whole character. Once we turn it on, you can see the module stepping here one at a time. You can see the hub rotating in there with the help of the stepper motor. You can also see at the top here the flaps are retained [music] by this faceplate so that only one flips down at a time. The type of motor you're using on a split-flap display is important. This is a stepper motor and it allows us to turn a precise number of degrees so that we can display whatever letter we want. The other half of the story is this little sensor here, which is called a Hall effect sensor. And when it rotates, it can detect this little magnet here so that when I plug it in, it's going to rotate until the magnet is in line with the Hall effect sensor, which is indicated by that LED there. And then after that, it knows precisely where it is. And if you want to type a C, for example, it knows how many degrees to rotate to show that C. So, this is just how one module of a split-flap display works, but imagine you had 50 of them. It gets more complicated as you add more of these and that's the project that I'll be working on in this video. All right. Now that we all know how a split-flap works, let me show you this one again. I'll take it apart, show you some of the things I like and dislike, and that way we'll get some requirements going for a new design. So, right off the bat, one thing I really like about this design is the size of the modules themselves. They're pretty compact. The motor is right inside of the hub, which I'll show you in a second. So, it allows them to be right up next to each other, but I don't like how many modules it has. It only has eight in this version and I definitely want mine to have a lot more than that. So, let me take it apart and show you some other things that I want to change on the inside. All right. Now that it's apart, you can see all these modules are strung together by connectors here and they're all held together by these long threaded rods that go through all eight modules. This is one thing I don't like cuz it's hard to put these together and requires you to, you know, sandwich all these wires together with these spacers and it was just a pain to put together and I don't think it's going to scale very well when I add more modules. So, we're definitely going to change that. Here's a closer view of one of the modules' enclosures and I really like most of this design. The motor is in here. Here's some gears that help turn the hub. There's a Hall effect sensor in the back and I like this wire retainer clip. So, we're definitely going to use this as a starting point. But, one thing I don't like too much is this electronics package here. It's a bit of a rat's nest of wires and it was hard to solder it all together and cram it in there. So, we're definitely going to try and improve upon that for my version. Our first criteria, I think, is that we want it to have more flaps. I like A through Z, 0 through 9, but [music] I want some special characters involved. I like the ones that involve some color. So, we're going to set it to 64 flaps. We also want it to have more modules in the first place. [music] I don't want just a single line. My goal is going to be 15 modules per row and three rows [music] for a total of 45 modules. Now, with that many modules, we need it to be much easier to build. [music] So, the goal is for it to be totally modular and essentially that means snap-on, snap-off design. I don't want to have any threaded rods throughout the entire thing. I don't want to deal with taking the whole [music] thing apart just to service one in the middle. I just want to be able to plug and play and have the modules working. [music] For criterion number four, I don't want to have to do too much soldering. I think there might be a little bit involved, but for the most part, I want the boards to be self-enclosed. I want to just be able to plug in the motor, plug in the sensor, and be ready to go. And the last criteria, might be the hardest to hit, is that I want to spend under $1,000 on this project. Online, I can buy a board that's 15 by 3 [music] for under $1,000. So, I think it would only be fitting that mine should cost less than that. It wouldn't make any sense for me to build one [music] just for it to be more expensive than the one that I could buy. So, that's my goal. That's my target that I'm going to hit. We'll keep a tally of how [music] much I spend and see if I get there in the end. All right. Let's tackle the easy part first. How are we going to make it have 64 flaps? Well, the current version has 37. So, to make it have 64, you might think you just need to add 27 more holes. But, it can't be that easy, can't it? Well, the short answer is maybe. These are the stepper motors that power the whole thing. They're little 28BYJ-48 5-V stepper motors and they don't have a great resolution. So, there's no guarantee they can handle 64 flaps. But, there's nothing to do than to just try it out. So, I went into Onshape, removed all the old holes, and extruded 64 new ones. But a bing, but a boom, you got a new drum. And after a quick 3D print, here's the old one and here's the new one. You can see the 27 new holes. They're a little bit smaller and I did have to modify the flaps to make them a little bit thinner and smaller to fit, but that's pretty much all I had to do. Just add the new characters [music] that we want and all we got to do is try it on a module and see if there's enough resolution to make it work. And thankfully, it did work. You can see it step along happily here and it gives me more confidence that these things have enough resolution to handle the 64 flaps. I guess it was just as easy as adding more holes to the drum.
